title: Syncing Your Data
description: How cloud sync works across devices and what happens when you're offline.

sections.body: Plantgotchi uses an offline-first architecture. Your phone is the primary data store, and the cloud is a sync layer that keeps everything accessible across devices.

sections.heading: How Sync Works
sections.body:
When your device is online, the app syncs with Turso — a distributed SQLite database in the cloud. Sync happens in two directions:

  • Push — sensor readings and care logs are uploaded from your phone to the cloud.
  • Pull — plant profiles and AI recommendations are downloaded to your phone.

The sync protocol uses Turso's HTTP pipeline API, batching multiple SQL statements into a single request for efficiency.

sections.heading: Conflict Resolution
sections.body:
What happens when two phones record data for the same plant? Plantgotchi handles this gracefully:

  • Sensor readings — deduplicated by a unique constraint on (sensor_id, timestamp). If two phones capture the same BLE reading, INSERT OR IGNORE keeps just one copy.
  • Care logs — each log has a UUID, so entries from different phones are both preserved (no conflict).
  • Plant edits — last push wins. If you rename a plant on two devices, the most recently synced name is kept.

sections.heading: Offline Mode
sections.body:
When you're offline, the app works normally:

  • BLE sensor readings continue to be captured and stored locally.
  • The offline rule engine still generates alerts instantly.
  • Care actions are logged to the local database.
  • All pending changes are queued and automatically synced when connectivity returns.

The only features that require internet are AI recommendations (from Claude) and accessing your garden from the web dashboard.

sections.heading: Sync Frequency
sections.body: The app syncs automatically when it detects a network connection. You can also trigger a manual sync by pulling down on the garden screen. AI recommendations run on a 6-hour cycle when online.
